enrichment.seo_meta_description: ShareFile is a secure client portal, file sharing, and e-signature platform for accounting, legal, and healthcare firms, from Progress Software.

enrichment.short_description: Shortcut is a project management platform built for software teams, combining stories, epics, and iterations with native Git integrations.
enrichment.headquarters: New York City, New York, USA

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Shortcut is a project management platform designed specifically for software development teams. Originally launched as Clubhouse in 2014, the company rebranded to Shortcut in 2021 after the rise of the unrelated Clubhouse audio app made the original name impractical, though the underlying product and its philosophy stayed consistent throughout the transition.

The core of Shortcut is built around a hierarchy that maps naturally to how engineering teams already think: stories, epics, and iterations. Work moves through customizable workflow states on Kanban-style boards, and teams can organize larger initiatives into epics while grouping day-to-day execution into time-boxed iterations, giving both granular task tracking and higher-level roadmap visibility in one connected system.

Headquartered in New York City, Shortcut operates a freemium pricing model: a free tier supports small teams of up to ten users, while paid Team, Business, and Enterprise tiers unlock unlimited seats, deeper reporting, and administrative controls. The company has more recently added AI-assisted capabilities, including an in-app agent called Korey aimed at helping engineering teams plan and manage work alongside automated assistance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Shortcut Is For
paragraphs: Shortcut is aimed squarely at software development teams rather than general business operations. Startups and small-to-midsize engineering organizations that want an agile tracking tool without the configuration overhead of larger enterprise suites tend to be the best fit, since the product favors sensible defaults over exhaustive customization.

Product managers and engineering leads who want a lightweight way to plan sprints, track epics, and report on velocity without a heavy administrative burden are a natural audience. Teams transitioning off spreadsheets, lightweight Kanban tools, or an overly complex issue tracker often cite simplicity as the reason they move to Shortcut.

Teams already living inside GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket benefit most, since Shortcut's Git integrations let commits, branches, and pull requests automatically update story status. This makes it especially appealing to engineering-first organizations that want project management to stay tightly coupled to the actual code being shipped.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Non-technical departments such as marketing, HR, sales, or general operations are unlikely to find Shortcut useful, since its data model and terminology (stories, epics, iterations) are built around software delivery workflows rather than generic task or campaign management.

Very large enterprises with complex cross-departmental governance, extensive compliance workflows, or the need for deeply configurable permission structures across hundreds of teams may find tools like Jira, Azure DevOps, or ServiceNow better suited to that scale, since Shortcut intentionally keeps its administrative surface simpler.

Organizations that need extensive no-code workflow building, elaborate custom field logic, or highly bespoke process automation beyond what Shortcut's built-in automations offer may hit limits. Teams whose primary requirement is maximum configurability over ease of use are likely to outgrow Shortcut's more opinionated approach.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At the center of Shortcut is its story, epic, and iteration structure, paired with customizable Kanban boards and backlog views. Teams can define multiple workflows for different squads, apply work-in-progress limits, and keep prioritized backlogs visible alongside active sprint work, all within the same shared workspace.

Integration with version control is a defining feature: GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket connections let branch names and pull requests automatically move stories through workflow states. On top of that, Automations reduce manual busywork, Custom Fields let teams tailor stories to their own processes, and Roadmaps provide a higher-level view of planned initiatives over time.

Reporting tools cover velocity, cycle time, and team throughput, while Objectives functionality lets organizations connect day-to-day story work to broader OKRs. A documented API supports custom integrations, and the newer Korey AI agent is positioned to help teams plan, triage, and manage work with less manual overhead.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Shortcut Experience
paragraphs: Shortcut's interface is deliberately fast and keyboard-friendly, a design choice reflected in the product's own name. Common actions like creating stories, moving cards between states, and searching across the workspace are optimized to be done without reaching for the mouse, which resonates with developer users in particular.

New teams typically report a short onboarding curve. Setting up a workspace, connecting a Git provider, and configuring a first workflow can be done in a single sitting, without the lengthy administrative configuration that larger enterprise tools often require before a team can start tracking real work.

Overall, the day-to-day experience leans developer-first: clean visual design, minimal clutter, and a workflow model that mirrors how engineering teams already talk about their work. Compared to more configurable enterprise tools, Shortcut trades some flexibility for speed and a noticeably lower learning curve.

features.title: Stories, Epics and Iterations
features.description: Organize work using a hierarchy built for software teams, connecting granular tasks to larger initiatives and time-boxed sprints.

features.title: Kanban Boards and Backlogs
features.description: Visualize work across customizable workflow states while keeping a prioritized backlog of upcoming stories.

features.title: Git Integrations
features.description: Connect GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket so branches, commits, and pull requests automatically update story status.

features.title: Automations
features.description: Reduce manual upkeep by automating routine actions like status changes and notifications.

features.title: Roadmaps
features.description: Plan and communicate larger initiatives over time with a high-level view above day-to-day execution.

features.title: Objectives (OKRs)
features.description: Link team-level story work to broader company objectives and key results for alignment tracking.

features.title: Reporting and Analytics
features.description: Measure velocity, cycle time, and throughput to review team progress over sprints and quarters.

features.title: Custom Fields
features.description: Tailor stories and workflows to a team's own processes with flexible, configurable fields.

features.title: API Access
features.description: Build custom integrations and extend Shortcut's data model using a documented API.

features.title: Korey AI Agent
features.description: An in-app AI agent designed to assist with planning, triage, and day-to-day work management.

pros: Purpose-built data model (stories, epics, iterations) that maps naturally to how software teams already work
Tight, native integrations with GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket link code activity directly to project tracking
Free tier available for small teams, lowering the barrier to trying the product
Generally regarded as faster to set up and simpler to learn than heavier enterprise tools
Keyboard-friendly, developer-oriented interface designed for speed over deep configuration menus
cons: Not designed for non-technical departments or general-purpose project management outside software teams
Fewer deep customization and governance options than large enterprise tools like Jira or Azure DevOps
Free plan is capped at a small number of users, limiting its use for larger teams
Advanced reporting and administrative controls require moving to higher-priced paid tiers
Smaller ecosystem of third-party integrations and marketplace apps compared to more established competitors

faqs.question: Is Shortcut free to use?
faqs.answer: Shortcut offers a free plan for teams of up to 10 users with core Kanban, roadmap, and basic reporting features. Larger teams or those needing advanced features must move to a paid Team, Business, or Enterprise plan.

faqs.question: Was Shortcut previously known as Clubhouse?
faqs.answer: Yes. The product launched as Clubhouse in 2014 and was renamed Shortcut in 2021, largely to avoid confusion with the unrelated audio social app also called Clubhouse.

faqs.question: Does Shortcut integrate with GitHub or GitLab?
faqs.answer: Yes. Shortcut has native integrations with GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket that let commits, branches, and pull requests automatically update the status of linked stories.

faqs.question: Who is Shortcut best suited for?
faqs.answer: Shortcut is built for software development teams, including engineers, product managers, and engineering leads, particularly small-to-midsize teams that want agile tracking without heavy administrative overhead.

faqs.question: Does Shortcut offer a free trial for paid plans?
faqs.answer: Yes. Shortcut provides a full-access free trial (no credit card required) so teams can evaluate paid-tier features before committing to a subscription.

use_cases.title: Agile sprint planning for engineering teams
use_cases.description: Teams break down epics into stories, assign them to iterations, and track progress across Kanban workflow states in real time.

use_cases.title: Connecting code activity to project tracking
use_cases.description: Engineering teams link Git branches and pull requests to stories so status updates automatically as code moves through review and merge.

use_cases.title: Roadmap and OKR alignment
use_cases.description: Product managers use Roadmaps and Objectives to connect day-to-day story work with quarterly company goals and communicate progress to stakeholders.

use_cases.title: Lightweight issue tracking for small startups
use_cases.description: Early-stage teams adopt the free tier to manage backlogs and sprints without the setup overhead of larger enterprise project tools.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Shortcut is a project management platform for software teams, offering stories, epics, Git integrations, and a free tier for small teams.

enrichment.short_description: Cloud-based e-signature and document workflow platform from airSlate for signing, sending, and managing legally binding documents.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: SignNow is a cloud-based electronic signature and document workflow platform owned by airSlate, built to let individuals and organizations sign, send, and manage legally binding documents from a browser or mobile device. It launched in 2011 and has grown into one of the more affordably positioned players in an e-signature market otherwise dominated by higher-priced enterprise incumbents such as DocuSign and Adobe Acrobat Sign.

At its core, SignNow lets users upload a document, add signature and data fields, and route it to one or more signers, tracking status in real time until every party has completed their part. Reusable templates, bulk sending, and document groups extend this beyond one-off signing into repeatable workflows for onboarding, sales agreements, procurement, and internal approvals, so teams aren't rebuilding the same document layout every time it needs to go out.

As part of airSlate's broader no-code workflow suite, SignNow can be paired with adjacent airSlate products for document generation, PDF editing, and workflow automation, giving growing teams a path to more advanced document processes without switching vendors. That said, it also functions perfectly well as a standalone e-signature tool for teams that only need signing and basic document routing rather than a full workflow automation platform, which keeps the learning curve manageable for smaller organizations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who SignNow Is For
paragraphs: SignNow is best suited to small and mid-sized businesses that need dependable e-signature functionality without paying enterprise e-signature prices. Its per-user, per-month pricing, rather than per-envelope or per-signature pricing on most tiers, makes costs predictable for teams that send a steady but moderate volume of documents each month.

Sales teams, HR departments, real estate agents, and legal or operations staff who regularly send contracts, offer letters, NDAs, and agreements tend to get the most value, particularly once they set up reusable templates and bulk-send workflows so the same document doesn't have to be rebuilt for every recipient. Remote and distributed teams also benefit, since the entire signing process happens online without anyone needing to print, scan, or mail paperwork.

It also appeals to organizations already invested in the broader airSlate ecosystem, or to budget-conscious teams evaluating alternatives to DocuSign or Adobe Acrobat Sign, since SignNow's Business and Business Premium tiers typically undercut those competitors on list price while still covering the fundamentals of sending, signing, and tracking documents. Freelancers and solo practitioners who need occasional but professional-looking signing links can also make the entry-level plan work well.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: High-volume senders should read the fine print carefully: SignNow's plans, including Enterprise, cap included signature invites per user per year, with additional sends billed per invite. Organizations that send documents in large batches regularly, such as staffing agencies or high-volume retail operations, may find this cap adds unpredictable cost on top of the subscription fee once they exceed the allotment.

Teams that need the deepest workflow automation, document generation, or advanced conditional-logic capabilities may outgrow SignNow's native feature set and end up needing to add other airSlate products or look at more workflow-centric platforms altogether, since SignNow itself is scoped primarily around signing and light document routing rather than full business-process automation.

There is no permanent free plan, only a time-limited free trial, so anyone looking for an indefinite no-cost e-signature tool for very occasional personal use will need to look elsewhere or budget for a paid subscription once the trial ends. Organizations with strict, highly specialized compliance requirements should also confirm SignNow meets their specific regulatory needs before committing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: SignNow's core toolset covers legally binding e-signatures, drag-and-drop field placement for signatures, initials, dates, text, and checkboxes, reusable templates, and bulk sending so a single document can go out to many recipients at once. Document groups let related files be routed and tracked together as a single signing workflow instead of managing several separate documents by hand.

Higher tiers add business-oriented capabilities such as payment requests embedded in signed documents, custom branding on the signing experience, conditional fields that adapt a form based on earlier answers, signer attachments for supporting files, and audit trails with detailed activity and completion reporting for compliance and recordkeeping purposes.

SignNow also offers native iOS and Android apps for signing and sending on the go, an API and integrations with tools like Salesforce, Google Workspace, and popular cloud storage providers such as Dropbox and Box, and administrative controls for managing users, teams, and permissions across an organization's account. Qualified electronic signatures and additional compliance options are available on the top-tier Site License plan for regulated industries.

long_form_blocks.heading: The SignNow Experience
paragraphs: Day to day, SignNow's interface is oriented around speed: uploading a document, dropping in fields, and sending for signature is a fairly quick, guided process, and the mobile apps mirror this closely so documents can be reviewed and signed from a phone without much friction, which matters for signers who are on the move or reviewing paperwork outside the office.

The signing side of the experience is deliberately lightweight for recipients, who typically don't need an account to review and sign a document, which reduces drop-off compared to tools that force recipients through account creation before they can complete a signature. Notifications and reminders help keep pending documents from stalling in someone's inbox.

Administrators managing multiple users will spend more time in the account and team settings, where branding, user roles, and integration configuration live; this layer feels more utilitarian than polished, which is consistent with SignNow's positioning as a value-focused alternative rather than the most design-forward option in its category. Overall, the experience prioritizes getting documents signed quickly over visual flourish.

features.title: Legally binding e-signatures
features.description: Collect signatures that comply with major e-signature laws such as ESIGN and eIDAS, with a full audit trail for each completed document.

features.title: Reusable templates
features.description: Convert frequently sent documents into templates with pre-set fields so they can be reused without rebuilding the layout each time.

features.title: Bulk sending
features.description: Send the same document to a large list of recipients in one action, tracking each recipient's individual completion status.

features.title: Document groups
features.description: Bundle multiple related documents into a single workflow so signers complete them together as one packet.

features.title: In-document payment requests
features.description: Collect payment at the same time a document is signed, without needing a separate invoicing or checkout step.

features.title: Mobile apps
features.description: Native iOS and Android apps allow documents to be reviewed, signed, and sent from a mobile device.

features.title: API and integrations
features.description: Connect SignNow to CRMs, cloud storage, and other business apps such as Salesforce and Google Workspace, or build custom workflows via the API.

features.title: Custom branding
features.description: Apply company logos and branding to the signing experience on Business Premium and higher plans.

features.title: Advanced reporting and audit trails
features.description: Track document status, signer activity, and completion history for compliance and internal recordkeeping.

pros: Per-user pricing is generally lower than several major e-signature competitors at comparable feature levels
Simple, quick document setup and signing flow for both senders and recipients
Recipients typically don't need to create an account to sign, reducing completion friction
Native mobile apps make signing and sending usable from a phone or tablet
Bulk sending and document groups support repeatable, high-volume workflows
cons: Annual signature invite caps apply even on the top-tier Enterprise plan, with per-invite overage charges beyond that
No permanent free plan, only a time-limited free trial
Advanced workflow automation and document generation require pairing with other airSlate products
Some administrative and settings screens feel more utilitarian than polished
Higher-tier features like custom branding and conditional fields are gated behind Business Premium and Enterprise plans

faqs.question: Is SignNow free to use?
faqs.answer: SignNow does not offer a permanent free plan. It offers a time-limited free trial so users can test the platform before subscribing to one of its paid tiers.

faqs.question: Who owns SignNow?
faqs.answer: SignNow is owned by airSlate, a company that provides a broader suite of document workflow and automation tools alongside the SignNow e-signature product.

faqs.question: Are SignNow signatures legally binding?
faqs.answer: Yes, SignNow's e-signatures are designed to comply with major electronic signature laws, including ESIGN in the United States and eIDAS in the European Union, and each signed document includes an audit trail.

faqs.question: Does SignNow have a limit on how many documents I can send?
faqs.answer: Paid plans include a set number of signature invites per user per year, including on the Enterprise plan; sending beyond that allotment incurs an additional per-invite charge.

faqs.question: Does SignNow have mobile apps?
faqs.answer: Yes, SignNow offers native apps for iOS and Android so documents can be sent, reviewed, and signed from a mobile device.

use_cases.title: Sales contract signing
use_cases.description: Sales teams send proposals and contracts as reusable templates, track signer status in real time, and close deals faster without printing or scanning.

use_cases.title: HR onboarding paperwork
use_cases.description: HR departments send offer letters, NDAs, and onboarding forms as templates or document groups so new hires can complete everything in one signing session.

use_cases.title: Real estate transaction documents
use_cases.description: Agents and brokers send purchase agreements, disclosures, and lease documents for signature, using bulk sending when multiple parties need the same paperwork.

use_cases.title: Client agreements with payment collection
use_cases.description: Service businesses attach a payment request to a signed service agreement or invoice so the client pays at the same time they sign.

features: Set number of signature invites per user/year

enrichment.seo_meta_description: SignNow review: pricing, features, pros and cons of airSlate's e-signature platform for signing, sending, and managing documents online.
